---
title: "Hidden Markov Models"
subtitle: "Day 9 — Quantitative biology"
author: "Quantitative biology and integrative omics"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    highlight-style: github
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-line-numbers: false
    incremental: false
    chalkboard: false
    menu: true
---

## About this lecture

This is the **last and hardest** topic of the linear-algebra block. By the end
you will be able to:

- Explain why a **visible** Markov chain (Day 7) is not enough when the thing
  you care about is **hidden**.
- Name the four ingredients of a **Hidden Markov Model** (HMM): hidden states,
  a transition matrix, emission probabilities, and an initial distribution.
- Tell the **generative story** of an HMM and read off the probability of a
  single hidden path with its observations.
- State the **three classic problems** — likelihood, decoding, learning — and
  which algorithm solves each.
- Compute the probability of **one specific path** for a tiny 2-state HMM **by
  hand**.

Notation: hidden states with transition matrix $A$, emission matrix $B$,
initial distribution $\boldsymbol{\pi}$; hidden sequence $\mathbf{z}$,
observations $\mathbf{x}$.

::: notes
Welcome to day nine. This is the conceptual peak of the block, so we go slowly
and lead with biology. Everything builds directly on the Markov chains from
days seven and eight — the hidden layer of an HMM *is* exactly that chain. The
only genuinely new idea today is that we do not get to see the states; we see
noisy emissions instead. Today is concept and by-hand; tomorrow we fit HMMs in
R on real-looking genome data.
:::

## Recap: a visible Markov chain (Day 7) {.smaller}

A Markov chain has **states** and a **transition matrix** $A$ whose rows sum
to 1. The **Markov property**: the next state depends only on the current one.

Example over DNA bases — suppose we modelled which base follows which:

$$
A=\begin{array}{c c}
 & \begin{array}{cccc} \ \ A\ \ & \ \ C\ \ & \ \ G\ \ & \ \ T\ \ \end{array}\\
\begin{array}{c} A\\ C\\ G\\ T \end{array} &
\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
0.3 & 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.2\\
0.2 & 0.3 & 0.3 & 0.2\\
0.2 & 0.3 & 0.3 & 0.2\\
0.3 & 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.3
\end{array}\right]
\end{array}
$$

Crucially, in this chain **the state IS the letter you read**. You observe the
state directly.

::: notes
Remind them of the day-seven setup: a transition matrix with rows summing to
one, and the one-step update being a matrix-vector product. Here I have made
the *states* be the DNA letters themselves, so there is nothing hidden — you
read the sequence, you know the state. Hold onto that, because the whole point
of today is to break it: the states will become things we cannot read off
directly.
:::

## The biology: where is the CpG island? {.smaller}

Genomes are not uniform. Some stretches are **GC-rich** ("CpG-island-like",
often near gene starts); the rest is **normal** background.

You are handed a sequence:

```
... A T G C G C G C G G C C G C A T A T A A T T A C A ...
        |________ GC-rich? ________|  |__ normal? __|
```

You can **see the letters** (A, C, G, T). You **cannot see** the label
"GC-rich" vs "normal" — that label is the biology you actually want.

The letters are **emissions**. The hidden region label is the **hidden state**.

::: notes
This is the motivating pairing for both days. A CpG island is a genomic region
unusually rich in C and G; they often sit at promoters and matter for gene
regulation. The key observation: nature does not annotate the sequence for you.
You see A, C, G, T; you must *infer* which segments are islands. So there are
two layers — a hidden layer of region labels and a visible layer of bases — and
that is precisely a hidden Markov model.
:::

## Why the visible chain is not enough

If the label "GC-rich vs normal" were written in the sequence, we would just
read it — a plain Markov chain over labels (Day 7).

But it is **not** written down. We only get the letters. Two different hidden
states can emit the **same** letter:

- A "GC-rich" region emits a `G` *often*.
- A "normal" region emits a `G` *too*, just less often.

So a single `G` does not tell you the state. The state is **hidden**; we must
**infer** it from the pattern of emissions over a whole stretch.

::: notes
Hammer the limitation. A visible chain assumes you see the states. Here you do
not, and the emissions are ambiguous: every base can come from either state,
only with different probabilities. One letter is nearly useless; it is the
*run* of letters that betrays the hidden state. That inference problem is what
the HMM machinery is built to solve.
:::

## Two layers: the generative picture {.smaller}

An HMM tells a **generative story** in two coupled layers:

```
hidden:   z1  ->  z2  ->  z3  ->  z4     (a Markov chain: GC-rich / normal)
           |       |       |       |
           v       v       v       v
visible:  x1      x2      x3      x4      (emitted bases: A C G T)
```

- The **hidden** layer is a Day-7 Markov chain over states (here:
  `GC-rich`, `normal`), governed by transition matrix $A$.
- At **each** hidden state, you draw one **emission** (a base) from that
  state's emission distribution, governed by matrix $B$.

You only ever see the bottom row. The top row is hidden.

::: notes
This diagram is the mental model for the whole topic. Top row: the hidden chain
hops between GC-rich and normal according to A, exactly the Markov chain we
already know. Bottom row: every time we land in a hidden state, that state
"speaks" a letter, drawn from its own emission distribution B. The arrows down
are emissions; the arrows across are transitions. We observe only the bottom
row and must reason backwards to the top.
:::

## The four ingredients of an HMM {.smaller}

1. **Hidden states** — the things you cannot observe.
   Here: $\{\text{GC-rich},\ \text{normal}\}$.
2. **Transition matrix $A$** — how the hidden chain moves, rows sum to 1.
   $A_{ij}=P(z_{t+1}=j \mid z_t=i)$. *This is the Day-7 Markov chain.*
3. **Emission matrix $B$** — what each hidden state emits, rows sum to 1.
   $B_{ik}=P(x_t=k \mid z_t=i)$ (one row per state, one column per symbol).
4. **Initial distribution $\boldsymbol{\pi}$** — where the chain starts,
   $\pi_i=P(z_1=i)$, entries sum to 1.

An HMM is the triple $(A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi})$ plus the lists of states and
symbols.

::: notes
List the four ingredients and keep emphasising which is old and which is new.
A and pi are pure Markov-chain objects from day seven. The genuinely new piece
is B, the emission matrix: for every hidden state, a probability distribution
over the symbols you can observe. Note both A and B have rows that sum to one,
but they are different shapes — A is states-by-states, B is states-by-symbols.
:::

## A tiny concrete 2-state HMM {.smaller}

States $\{H, L\}$ — **H** = "GC-rich (high)", **L** = "normal (low)".
Symbols: the bases $\{A, C, G, T\}$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}=\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
A=\begin{array}{cc}
 & \begin{array}{cc} H & L \end{array}\\
\begin{array}{c} H\\ L \end{array} &
\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{array}\right]
\end{array}
$$

$$
B=\begin{array}{c}
 \begin{array}{cccc} \ A\  & \ C\  & \ G\  & \ T\  \end{array}\\
\begin{array}{c} \end{array}
\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
0.1 & 0.4 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\
0.3 & 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.3
\end{array}\right]
\end{array}
\begin{array}{c} H\\ L \end{array}
$$

Read $B$: state $H$ emits C or G with prob 0.4 each (GC-rich!); state $L$
spreads probability more evenly.

::: notes
This is the example we will compute with by hand, and again in R tomorrow, so
introduce it carefully. Pi says we start in either state with equal chance. A
says both states are sticky — H stays H with probability 0.8, L stays L with
0.7 — which encodes that islands and background come in runs. B is the new
object: the high state loves C and G, the low state is roughly uniform. Sanity
check every row sums to one.
:::

## The generative story, step by step {.smaller}

To **generate** a sequence of length 3 from this HMM:

1. Draw $z_1$ from $\boldsymbol{\pi}$. Say $z_1 = H$.
2. Emit $x_1$ from row $H$ of $B$. Say $x_1 = G$ (prob 0.4).
3. Move: draw $z_2$ from row $H$ of $A$. Say $z_2 = H$ (prob 0.8).
4. Emit $x_2$ from row $H$ of $B$. Say $x_2 = C$ (prob 0.4).
5. Move: draw $z_3$ from row $H$ of $A$. Say $z_3 = L$ (prob 0.2).
6. Emit $x_3$ from row $L$ of $B$. Say $x_3 = A$ (prob 0.3).

We **observe** only $\mathbf{x} = (G, C, A)$. The path
$\mathbf{z} = (H, H, L)$ is hidden.

::: notes
Walk through generation as a recipe, alternating transition and emission. Start
state from pi, emit, transition, emit, transition, emit. Each choice has a
probability attached. The student sees only the emitted letters G C A; the path
H H L is invisible. The next slide multiplies all those probabilities together,
which is the single most important by-hand skill for the exam.
:::

## Probability of ONE path with its emissions {.smaller}

The HMM is a product of independent choices, so multiply along the story.
For path $\mathbf{z}=(H,H,L)$ and observations $\mathbf{x}=(G,C,A)$:

$$
P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})
= \underbrace{\pi_H}_{\text{start}}\,
  \underbrace{B_{H,G}}_{\text{emit}}\,
  \underbrace{A_{H,H}}_{\text{move}}\,
  \underbrace{B_{H,C}}_{\text{emit}}\,
  \underbrace{A_{H,L}}_{\text{move}}\,
  \underbrace{B_{L,A}}_{\text{emit}}
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.8 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.003840.
$$

The pattern: **start prob → (emit, transition, emit, transition, …, emit)**.
One $\boldsymbol{\pi}$, then alternate $B$ and $A$, ending on an emission.

::: notes
This is THE formula to memorise. The joint probability of a hidden path and its
observations is just the product of: the initial probability, then for each
position the emission probability, and between positions the transition
probability. Count the factors: for length T you get one pi, T emissions, and
T minus one transitions. Do this multiplication slowly; on the exam this is
where marks are won. Note the answer is small — products of probabilities
shrink fast, which is why real implementations work in log space.
:::

## Three classic problems {.smaller}

Given an HMM and an observed sequence $\mathbf{x}$, three questions:

1. **Likelihood** — how probable is $\mathbf{x}$ under the model,
   $P(\mathbf{x})$? Summed over **all** hidden paths.
   → **Forward algorithm**.
2. **Decoding** — what is the single **most likely hidden path**
   $\mathbf{z}^\*$? → **Viterbi algorithm**. *(This is what predicts the
   islands.)*
3. **Learning** — given only unlabelled $\mathbf{x}$, estimate
   $(A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi})$. → **Baum–Welch** (an EM algorithm).

Today: the concepts. Tomorrow: all three in R.

::: notes
Frame the three problems as three different questions you might ask of the same
model and data. Likelihood scores the whole model; you need it to compare
models. Decoding gives you the annotation — the most probable sequence of
hidden labels — which is literally the predicted island map. Learning is the
hardest: estimating the parameters when you have only raw sequence and no
labels. Name the algorithm for each but do not derive them; that is beyond
scope. The R functions tomorrow are forward, viterbi, baumWelch.
:::

## Why summing over paths matters (likelihood) {.smaller}

The path $(H,H,L)$ is **one** way to produce $\mathbf{x}=(G,C,A)$. But
$(H,H,H)$, $(L,H,L)$, … could also produce it.

The **likelihood** of the data is the sum over **every** hidden path:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\text{all paths } \mathbf{z}} P(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}).
$$

For 2 states and length 3 that is $2^3 = 8$ paths — fine by hand. For a real
chromosome ($2^{\text{millions}}$ paths) it is hopeless by brute force; the
**forward algorithm** does it efficiently by reusing partial sums.

::: notes
Stress the distinction between the joint probability of a specific path, which
we just computed, and the likelihood of the data, which marginalises over all
possible paths. The naive sum has a number of terms exponential in the sequence
length, so you cannot enumerate it for a genome. The forward algorithm is the
clever bookkeeping that collapses this to a linear-time computation by carrying
forward partial probabilities — conceptually like reusing work, not unlike the
matrix iterations from day eight. We will only call it, not implement it.
:::

## Decoding: the most likely path (Viterbi) {.smaller}

Decoding asks for the **single best** hidden path:

$$
\mathbf{z}^\* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{z}} \; P(\mathbf{z}\mid \mathbf{x}).
$$

The **Viterbi algorithm** finds it efficiently (same trick as forward, but
keeping the best path instead of the total).

For islands: run Viterbi, then **read off the runs of `H`** — those are your
**predicted CpG islands**. This turns a probabilistic model into a concrete
genome annotation.

::: notes
Decoding is the payoff for biology. Viterbi returns one hidden-state label per
position; wherever it says H you have a predicted GC-rich segment, and the
maximal runs of H are your predicted islands. Mechanically Viterbi is the same
dynamic program as forward but takes a max instead of a sum and remembers which
choice gave it, so it can trace the best path back. Tomorrow the viterbi
function does this in one line.
:::

## Learning: where do A, B, π come from? {.smaller}

So far we **assumed** the numbers in $A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi}$. Where do they
come from?

- **If you have labelled data** (sequences with known island annotations):
  just **count** transitions and emissions and normalise. Easy.
- **If you only have raw sequence** (no labels — the usual case):
  **Baum–Welch** iterates "guess the hidden states, re-estimate the
  parameters, repeat" until it converges. It is the **EM algorithm** for HMMs.

Caveat: Baum–Welch finds a **local** optimum — the starting guess matters, and
results need a sanity check.

::: notes
Address the obvious question: who told us the matrices? With labelled data,
parameter estimation is just counting and normalising, like estimating any
conditional probability. The realistic case has no labels, and Baum-Welch — an
expectation-maximisation method — alternates between softly inferring the hidden
states and re-fitting the parameters. Flag two practical warnings now so they
are not surprised tomorrow: it only finds a local optimum, and it is sensitive
to initialisation.
:::

## HMM vs visible Markov chain — the contrast {.smaller}

| | Visible Markov chain (Day 7) | Hidden Markov Model (today) |
|--|--|--|
| States | **observed** directly | **hidden** — must be inferred |
| Parameters | transition matrix $A$ | $A$ **and** emission matrix $B$ |
| You see | the states | only the **emissions** $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Core question | how does the chain evolve? | what hidden states explain what I see? |
| Biology | base-to-base model | island vs background **segmentation** |

An HMM = a Day-7 Markov chain on the hidden layer **plus** an emission layer.

::: notes
This table is the one-slide summary of the whole conceptual jump. The chain
machinery is identical; the addition is the emission layer and the fact that
states are no longer observed. If a student leaves understanding only this
table and the path-probability formula, today succeeded. Re-emphasise that
everything from days seven and eight still applies to the hidden layer.
:::

## Summary

- A genome's **island vs background** label is **hidden**; you see only the
  **bases**. That is exactly an HMM.
- An HMM = **hidden states** + transition matrix $A$ (the Day-7 chain) +
  **emission matrix** $B$ + initial distribution $\boldsymbol{\pi}$.
- **Generative story:** start from $\boldsymbol{\pi}$, then alternate
  *emit* ($B$) and *move* ($A$). The probability of one path-with-emissions is
  the **product** of those factors.
- **Three problems:** likelihood (**forward**), decoding (**Viterbi**),
  learning (**Baum–Welch**).

**Next:** Day 10 — fit and decode these HMMs in R with the `HMM` package on
genome-like sequence.

::: notes
Recap the four beats: the biology motivation, the four ingredients, the
generative product formula, and the three problems with their algorithms. The
exercise drills the by-hand path probability and the conceptual contrast; the
quiz checks the vocabulary, since this is the hardest day. Tomorrow we stop
assuming the matrices and let R both decode and learn them, then point forward
to PCA, Bayesian stats, and single-cell in the weeks ahead.
:::
